overview: The Teaching and learning area encompasses a variety of activities through which universities design, deliver, and foster students‘ learning experience. In particular, this includes lecture preparation, course design, classroom interaction, tutoring, feedback, assessment and collaborative projects. Teaching and learning in a digital learning environment (DLE), hence, require a shift from traditional methods toward new pedagogical approaches designed specifically for digital contexts. Instead of simply transferring conventional practices online, a new methodology should be developed that leverages interactivity, collaboration, and personalization as core principles. Digital platforms enable dynamic learning experiences through multimedia resources, virtual teamwork, and flexible self-paced study, making education more engaging and accessible. Within this framework, Artificial Intelligence plays a transformative role by supporting adaptive learning systems, providing real-time feedback, automating routine tasks, and offering educators data-driven insights. AI offers opportunities to personalize learning pathways, enrich and gamify course content, automate routine tasks, provide real-time support, and improve student engagement. Properly deployed, AI can empower educators to shift their focus from repetitive tasks to high-value interactions, focusing more on mentoring and fostering critical thinking. At the same time, transparency, academic integrity and inclusivity must remain central to ensure AI enhances, rather than replaces, the human dimension of education. In line with Alfa Labs’ vision of driving responsible innovation, the integration of Artificial Intelligence in education must be thus guided by strong ethical principles. While AI enables personalization, efficiency, and scalability, it also raises critical concerns such as data privacy, transparency, accountability, and potential bias. To ensure that digital transformation truly benefits all learners, it is essential to develop clear ethical guidelines, promote digital literacy, and maintain human oversight of AI-driven systems. By embedding fairness, inclusivity, and trust at the core of its methodology, Alfa Labs positions AI not only as a powerful tool for teaching and learning but also as a catalyst for shaping an equitable and future-ready educational ecosystem. instructions.maturity_scoring: For each item/statement in the following table, select: [0] Not in place | [1] Partially in place | [2] Fully in place providing also clear evidence for the suggested score and owner responsible for this item. Examples for evidence and owner are provided within the table below. Note that items which are marked as Critical need to be implemented before approaching a higher maturity level (necessary/minimum conditions).

items.item: AI-generated learning resources (summaries, visualizations, simulations) integrated into courses

score_anchors.0: No use of AI- generated resources.

items.evidence_examples: Course syllabi, sample resources, faculty reports

items.item: AI-based tutoring and chatbots for answering student questions outside class

score_anchors.2: Available and used in multiple programs.

items.evidence_examples: Chatbot logs, student feedback, usage analytics

items.item: Adaptive learning systems that personalize pace, content, and difficulty
items.evidence_examples: Platform usage data, student progress reports
items.owners: Center for Informati on Systems / Teachers

items.item: AI-driven support for inclusive education (e.g., accessibility tools, translation, voice-to-text)
items.evidence_examples: Accessibili ty tool logs, student participatio n data

items.item: Integration of AI into collaborative and project-based learning platforms

score_anchors.2: Systematic use in group work/project platforms.

items.item: Monitoring and analytics of student engagement and learning progress
Transparency and explanation of AI-supported learning tools for teachers and students

score_anchors.2: Clear, accessible explanations and guidance for all users.

items.evidence_examples: Guidance documents, training materials, student/tea cher surveys
items.owners: Departme nt of Quality and Ethics

score_ranges.interpretation: AI use in teaching is sporadic and experimental. Activities rely on isolated pilots or individual teacher initiatives without institutional coordination, guidance, or ethical oversight.

FAI-supported teaching tools and adaptive learning systems are increasingly implemented across faculties. Foundational policies, accessibility measures, and training are in place, though application remains uneven and teacher confidence varies.

AI is systematically embedded in teaching, learning, and assessment. Adaptive platforms, accessibility tools, and analytics dashboards are fully operational. Teachers and students are AI-literate, transparency is ensured, and pedagogy is data-informed, ethical, and inclusive.

build_action_plan.intro: Before approaching a higher level of maturity in the Teaching and learning practices AI DTP area, the critical items from 3.2.2 need to be fully implemented. For each unmet critical item, the necessary action needs to be specified to be performed (see table of foundational actions below).

foundational_actions.action: Integrate at least one AI- generated learning resource into pilot courses and document outcomes.
Deploy adaptive learning or analytics systems in at least one course and review effectiveness.
Ensure AI-driven accessibility tools are available and promoted to students with diverse needs.
Provide clear, accessible explanations and guidance for all AI-supported learning tools.

Emerging.action: Map current teaching practices and identify areas where AI could add immediate value (tutoring chatbots, pedagogical resource creation).
Emerging.owners: Pedagogical Experts / Center for Information Systems

Emerging.action: Pilot one AI-powered tool (e.g. chatbot for student FAQs) within a selected course.
Provide introductory workshops for teachers to build trust and familiarity with AI tools.

Established.action: Expand use of adaptive learning platforms to multiple courses and faculties.
Introduce AI-assisted lesson design tools to help educators diversify formats (quizzes, case studies, interactive media).
Train faculty to critically evaluate AI- generated content and adapt it to pedagogical goals.
Embed AI-supported accessibility services (translation, text-to-speech, captioning) into teaching and learning practices.
Explore and implement new pedagogical methodologies that combine traditional and AI-enhanced learning environments.

Enhanced.action: Co-create AI-enhanced curricula, where students also learn to critically use AI tools in learning.
Implement real-time analytics dashboards for teachers to track class engagement and adapt teaching methods.
Establish peer-learning networks supported by AI moderation and matching systems.
Share best practices across faculties and organise university-wide events on innovative AI-enhanced teaching.

kpis.intro: The following table presents key performance indicators (KPIs) for this area and target values required to achieve each maturity level. For each KPI, the corresponding owner should be specified.

items.name: Percentage of courses within selected Study Program using at least one AI- supported teaching tool
Average reduction in teacher time (reported via surveys or a collected feedback) spent on routine preparation tasks
items.owners: Teachers / Center for Teaching Competence Development

items.name: Student satisfaction with AI- enhanced learning experiences (positive survey responses)
Increase in participation of students with diverse needs through AI accessibility tools
Teacher satisfaction with AI support in teaching (survey)
Number of interdisciplinary courses or modules co-designed with AI-supported methods
